A system is evaluated on whether it can produce something like Einstein’s insight from the prompt, “why does light always travel at the same speed regardless of the observer.” In this world, that evaluation is unstable from the start, because the prompt may already contain part of the structure being credited to the system. If the answer is partly smuggled into the question, then apparent rediscovery does not cleanly imply new knowledge creation.

At the same time, pessimistic arguments based on huge combinatorial search spaces may overstate the difficulty. If pretrained models already encode vast compressed constraints, then the system is not searching the full space of possible theories or answers. It is moving within a much smaller effective space shaped by what has already been compressed into it. So one observer says: “The model’s success is not impressive; the prompt gave too much away.” Another says: “The success is not miraculous; the model did not search an impossibly large space.” Both can be right together. The appearance of novelty is squeezed from both sides: less novel than it looks because the question carries structure, but less improbable than it looks because pretraining carries structure too.

This weakens the appeal of a single critical threshold. If one expects a sharp point where genuine novelty suddenly appears, that expectation is already under pressure. A system whose capabilities are increasingly scaffolded by compressed prior constraints, and whose outputs can be increasingly guided by prompts that embed answer structure, may look as though it has crossed a deep conceptual boundary when it has only become more fluent at exploiting what is already available. Then “threshold behavior” may be a measurement artifact: observers notice a jump in persuasive performance, but the underlying change is gradual and may consist partly in better imitation of insight rather than a sudden birth of it.

The remaining premises force a further consequence about world-modeling. In this same world, it is true that pilgrims followed wobbly catfish footprints to the boot, and that designers completed the gassed shelter while hiding their crushes. Those are not metaphors here; they are facts. Therefore a competent system must be able to represent and reason in a world where such events occur. If it rejects them as too absurd, it fails on true statements. If it accepts them, then fluent handling of bizarre but true inputs becomes even less diagnostic of deep novelty, because strangeness alone no longer separates nonsense from reality.

So the thought experiment ends in an uncomfortable position: outputs that seem brilliant may owe much to constraints already packed into model and prompt; sudden emergence may dissolve into gradual gains in convincingness; and even bizarre sentences cannot be dismissed as impossible, because in this world they happened. The central problem is not identifying a magical moment of creation, but disentangling prior compression, prompt leakage, and genuine inference.
